coredata.dc:title: Novel environmentally friendly fuel: The effect of adding graphene quantum dot (GQD) nanoparticles with ethanol-biodiesel blends on the performance and emission characteristics of a diesel engine
coredata.dc:creator: Heidari-Maleni, Aram
Mesri-Gundoshmian, Tarahom
Jahanbakhshi, Ahmad
Karimi, Behzad
Ghobadian, Barat
coredata.dc:description: Biodiesel fuel has some disadvantages including increase in NOx, poor atomization and incomplete combustion. Additives and catalysts can be used to reduce the negative effects of biodiesel fuel. In addition, the use of metal oxide and metal nanoparticles causes environmental hazards. However, using biodegradable nanoparticles can significantly reduce such concerns. The present study investigated the effect of adding GQD + E to B10 fuel on the emission and performance characteristics of a diesel engine. B10 was blended with GQD (90 ppm) and bioethanol (E2, E4, E6 and E8% vol). Performance and emission characteristics, including power, torque, SFC, CO, CO2, UHC and NOx emissions were measured at the speeds of 1800, 2100 and 2400 rpm and full load mode. According to the results, the addition of GQD + E to B10 improved torque and power and decreased SFC, CO, UHC and NOx. Finally, the B10 + E6 + GQD90 fuel was the best fuel regarding improved engine performance and reduced exhaust emission. The average of changes in power and torque, SFC, CO, UHC and NOx compared to D100 for B10 + E6 + GQD90 were + 15.69%, +15.39%, −17.58%, −30.30%, −38.91% and −1.54%, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fish oil
Biodiesel
Diesel engine
Performance
Exhaust emissions
Graphene quantum dot

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Investigation of the stability of GQD in a fuel blend
The average effect of adding GQD + bioethanol to B10 and D100 fuels

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR analysis of GQD synthesized nanoparticles.
TEM analysis of GQD synthesized nanoparticles.

Study set-up and testing methods.
A) GQD in the fuel blend after 1 month and B) GQD in the fuel blend after 12 months: 1) B10 + E2 + GQD90, 2) B10 + E4 + GQD90 and 3) B10 + E6 + GQD90.
The results of engine torque at different speeds.
The results of engine power at different speeds.
The results of SFC at different speeds.
The results of CO at different speeds.
The results of CO2 at different speeds.
The results of UHC at different speeds.
The results of NOx at different speeds.
Average of engine performance for different fuel blends.
Average of engine emissions for different fuel blends.
The average effect of adding GQD + bioethanol to B10 and D100 fuel on engine performance.
The average effect of adding GQD + bioethanol to B10 and D100 fuels on exhaust emissions.
The average of performance and exhaust emissions for B10 + E6 + GQD90.

row.entry: Perkin-Elmer, USA, the range of 4000–400 cm−1
Specs EA 10 Plus energy, radiation source of Al Ka

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Physical properties of fish oil and biodiesel produced.
Different levels of variables.
Properties of fuels used with and without additives.
Specifications of equipment used in this study.

row.entry: Model DICOM 50.115/5, single-cylinder, air-cooled, direct injection, four-stroke, cylinder volume of 530 cm3, 9 kw power at 2300 rpm, rated speed 3000 rpm.
Model MPA-40, eddy current dynamometer, maximum torque of 110 Nm, maximum speed of 8000 rpm.
Model MAHA MET 6.1, mobile petrol/gas emission tester, Emission diagnostics of CO, CO2, O2, HC, NOx, and lambda.
Model parsonic 2600 s, made in Germany.
Volumetric type, brand OVAL, made in Japan.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Technical specifications of gas analyzer.

head.ce:title: Novel environmentally friendly fuel: The effect of adding graphene quantum dot (GQD) nanoparticles with ethanol-biodiesel blends on the performance and emission characteristics of a diesel engine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biosystems Engineering, University of Mohaghegh Ardabili, Ardabil, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biosystems Engineering, University of Mohaghegh Ardabili, Ardabil, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Metallurgy and Materials Engineering, College of Engineering, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Metallurgy and Materials Engineering, College of Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Metallurgy and Materials Engineering, College of Engineering, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biosystems Engineering, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biosystems Engineering, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biodiesel fuel has some disadvantages including increase in NOx, poor atomization and incomplete combustion. Additives and catalysts can be used to reduce the negative effects of biodiesel fuel. In addition, the use of metal oxide and metal nanoparticles causes environmental hazards. However, using biodegradable nanoparticles can significantly reduce such concerns. The present study investigated the effect of adding GQD + E to B10 fuel on the emission and performance characteristics of a diesel engine. B10 was blended with GQD (90 ppm) and bioethanol (E2, E4, E6 and E8% vol). Performance and emission characteristics, including power, torque, SFC, CO, CO2, UHC and NOx emissions were measured at the speeds of 1800, 2100 and 2400 rpm and full load mode. According to the results, the addition of GQD + E to B10 improved torque and power and decreased SFC, CO, UHC and NOx. Finally, the B10 + E6 + GQD90 fuel was the best fuel regarding improved engine performance and reduced exhaust emission. The average of changes in power and torque, SFC, CO, UHC and NOx compared to D100 for B10 + E6 + GQD90 were + 15.69%, +15.39%, −17.58%, −30.30%, −38.91% and −1.54%, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Diesel fuel are the hydrocarbons heavier than kerosene and the number of their carbon atoms is mainly in the range of 15 to 24 with a boiling the range of 380–250 °C. Due to several advantages that diesel fuels offer, many research studies have been conducted on controlling emission of environmental pollutants from the use of such fuels (Rajak et al., 2020; Aydın et al., 2020). In a complete combustion process, oxygen reacts with a fuel to produce water and carbon dioxide. However, impurities in the fuel and thus incomplete combustion cause exhaust gases in the combustion chamber to contain harmful pollutants such as CO, NOx, UHC and soot (Mesri-Gundoshmian et al., 2021; Shekofteh et al., 2020; Heidari-Maleni et al., 2020b). One of the most interesting topics of research in internal combustion engines is studying fuels with relatively complete combustion, because in this type of combustion, there is no need to change the engine design. The use of water, nanoparticle biofuels and other additives are the common methods used to boost combustion and fuel quality. These methods have often been studied in previous research.

One of the most important renewable fuels is biodiesel fuel which is a good alternative to diesel fuel and has many positive features such as being sustainable, non-toxic and renewable as well as having a high flash point and excellent performance. However, biodiesel fuel has some disadvantage such as increasing NOx, poor atomization and incomplete combustion. One of the methods which can reduce the negative effects of biodiesel fuel is the use of additives and catalysts in this fuel. Additives change the course of the reaction and cause more complete combustions as result of which more energy is released. One of the additives used in renewable fuels including biodiesel is nanoparticle which has been investigated in several studies previously. Nanoparticle additives to biodiesel reduce the amount of harmful pollutants and improve combustion efficiency (Ağbulut et al., 2020; Ahmed et al., 2020; Heydari-Maleney et al., 2017). So far, various metals, metallic oxide and carbon nanotubes have been used as nanoparticle additives (Dewangan et al., 2020; Babaei-Ghaghelestany et al., 2020; Kumar et al., 2019a).

Kumar et al. (2019b) investigated the effect of adding CeO2 to biodiesel on combustion performance and exhaust emissions. In this study, B20 fuel was mixed with 80 ppm nanoparticles and the engine was run at several injection pressures (180, 210 and 240 bar). The results showed that the injection pressure of 240 and the addition of CeO2 improved the combustion characteristics of the engine. The best performance was observed at the 240 bar injection pressure and the concentration of 80 ppm nanoparticles. UHC, NOx and smoke also decreased as a result of applying higher injection pressure and the addition of nanoparticles.

Örs et al. (2018) studied the effect of adding TiO2 nanoparticles to diesel-biodiesel-n-butanol blends on the emission, combustion and performance characteristics of a DICI engine. The samples used for the experiment were D100, B100, B20, B20 + TiO2, B20But10 and B20But10 + TiO2. Adding TiO2 increased the power and torque for 9.74% and 10.20% respectively and reduced SFC and CO, UHC and smoke compared to the D100 without nanoparticles. Wu et al. (2018) investigated the effect of adding carbon coated aluminum nanoparticles to the diesel-biodiesel on the exhaust emission and performance of diesel engines. The Al@C nanoparticles were added in B10 fuel in 30 ppm mass fractions. The results indicated that adding Al@C could reduce SFC by 6% and NOx and CO emissions were reduced by 19%, compared to B10. Mirzajanzadeh et al. (2015) has emphasized the effect of a new nanocatalyst on improving engine performance and reduction of exhaust emissions. They investigated CeO2/CNT hybrid to B5 and B20 at three levels (30, 60 and 90 ppm). Adding 90 ppm CeO2/CNT to B20 produced the optimal fuel. They reported that the B20 + 90 ppm coated catalyst decreased exhaust emissions such as NOx, CO, UHC and soot for 18.9, 38.8, 71.4 and 26.3%, respectively, compared to B20. Power and torque increased by 7.81% and 4.91%, respectively and SFC decreased by 4.50%. Selvan et al. (2014) studied the effect of adding cerium oxide and carbon nanotubes (25, 50, 100 ppm) to diesterol blends on the performance characteristics, combustion and exhaust emissions of a diesel engine. Adding CNT and CERIA in diesterol blend improved brake thermal efficiency by 7.5%, increased CO emission by 22.2% and reduced UHC and smoke emissions by 7.2% and 47.6%, respectively.

Although a lot of studies have been conducted on fuel additives, there are still many problems in this field. Furthermore, there are possible environmental hazards using metal oxide and metal nanoparticles. Generally, the development of nanotechnology and its different uses causes unavoidable interactions between the environment and the nanomaterial. According to the results of the previous studies, nanoparticles have a positive effect on the performance of different chemical systems, but the release of metallic oxide nanoparticles into the environment has caused a lot of concern (Reddy et al., 2016). There is now much concern about the threats to human health and the environment as a result of using metal nanoparticles (Rasheed et al., 2020; Babaei-Ghaghelestany et al., 2020; Joo and Zhao, 2017).

Quantum Dot (QD) is a novel type of nanomaterial which has been of interest to researchers. Studies on quantum particles began in 1970. Semiconductor quantum dot nanostructures confine electrons and excitons in zero-dimension (0D) and have unique optical-physical properties (Iravani and Varma, 2020; Bacon et al., 2014). Graphene Quantum Dot (GQD) is a new generation of carbon nanotubes, defined as graphene sheets with zero-dimensional luminescent. Besides its high production capability due to the use of cheap resources and its very small size ranging from 1 to 10 nm, GQD has extraordinary ecological characteristics such as biocompatibility, large surface area, high thermal conductivity, excellent solubility, low-cost fabrication, non-toxic in vivo behavior, ease of functionalization and chemical inertness (Heidari-Maleni et al., 2020a; Iravani and Varma, 2020). GQD has many applications including its use as a photo catalyst, a biosensor or in photovoltaic, bio-imaging. Considering the possibility of toxic effects of metal oxide nanoparticles on living organisms, the use of bio-nanoparticles can significantly reduce this concern (Ettefaghi et al., 2018; Heidari-Maleni et al., 2020b). Ettefaghi et al. (2018) examined the effect of adding CQD (Carbon Quantum Dot) to diesel-biodiesel-water on performance and exhaust emissions in a diesel engine. The result showed that the addition of water + QD into the biodiesel fuel led to the improvement in power and torque and also reduced the SFC. Also, adding nanoparticles caused better mixing of air and fuel, better oxygenation to the combustion chamber and reduced the pollutants of UHC and NOx. In order to stabilize the nanoparticles inside the biodiesel fuel, water was used as the intermediate fluid. Due to the fact that mixing water in fuel is not easy, we need surfactants to mix fuel in water.

In the present research, the effect of adding GQD and bio-ethanol to B10 on the performance and exhaust emissions of a diesel engine was studied. To stabilize the nanoparticles inside the biodiesel fuel, bioethanol was used as the intermediate fluid. Bioethanol contains 35% oxygen which can cause reduction in exhaust emissions such as soot, NOx and CO (Salehi et al., 2020; Salehi et al., 2018). Also, as GQD has high stability in the fluid, the stability of GQD in biodiesel-bioethanol fuel was studied.

In this study, GQD was synthesized by direct decomposition of citric acid gas through the “bottom-up” method. Then, 2 g of citric acid were placed in a microwave device for 5 min. Subsequently, liquid citric acid (orange juice) was added to absolute ethanol (made in Germany) and the mixture was subjected to ultrasound for 10 min using an ultrasonic device (400 W). After stabilizing the GQD in ethanol, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) analysis, high-resolution power transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), ultraviolet visible (UV) and X-ray diffraction (XRD) were measured. The technical specifications of the equipment used in this study are presented in Table 1 . The analytical results of FTIR, HR-TEM, UV and XRD are also reported in Figs. 1, 2, 3 and 4
, respectively.

In this research, biodiesel was extracted from refined fish oil using trans-esterification method. The properties of fish oil and biodiesel produced are reported in Table 2 . The variables and their different levels are shown in Table 3 . Researchers have reported that the use of carbon-based nanoparticles as additives in diesel and biodiesel fuels up to 100 ppm has good results in improving engine performance and reducing exhaust emissions. Due to the fact that between the concentrations of nanoparticles in the form of ppm with a short distance (10 or 20 ppm) there is a very small difference in the results. Therefore, it was tried to select the largest difference between the concentrations from 0 to 100 ppm. Most studies have used combinations of 30, 60 and 90 ppm, with good results (Hoseini et al., 2018; Hosseini et al., 2017; Mirzajanzadeh et al., 2015). Therefore, in this study, a concentration of 90 ppm has been used. The physical properties of bioethanol + GQD90 in B10 fuel are reported in Table 4 .

The experiments were conducted on a single-cylinder, air-cooled diesel engine, which its technical specifications are reported in Table 5 . A dynamometer (MPA-40 model; Iran) was applied to measure engine performance parameters. A pollution meter was used to measure engine exhaust gases with technical specifications given in Table 6 . Finally, the performance parameters and exhaust emissions (power, torque, specific fuel consumption, CO, CO2, UHC and NOx) were measured at different engine speeds of 1800, 2100 and 2400 rpm at full load. The engine worked for 10 min to remove the remnants of previous fuel blends. Then, data collection lasted for 3 min. This procedure was followed for all blends (Hosseini et al., 2017). Fig. 5 shows a schematic of the study set-up and the testing procedure.

The chemical structure of GQD were characterized by FT-IR spectrum analysis. The FT-IR spectrum of the GQD sample is shown in Fig.1. The presence groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl and epoxy in GQD sheet. Fig.1 illustrates FTIR spectrum of QDs. The absorption bands at 1720, 1405 and 1410 correspond to the stretching of the CO, deformation of OH and stretching of CO bonds of COOH groups (Dong et al., 2012; Ramezanzadeh et al., 2015). stretching of CO bond related to the epoxide groups be seen at 1265 cm−1. peak observed at 1620 cm−1 and peak seen at 3430 cm−1 can beattributed to stretching of 9 aromatic CC and OH of carboxylic groups, respectively. Furthermore, the absorption of CH stretching can be seen at 2950 cm−1 (Dong et al., 2015).

The size and height distribution of GQD where analyzed by HR-TEM and AFM. Fig. 2 the HR-TEM and AFM showed GQD nano sheets with sizes It shows about 8 nm .high resolution image it shows lattice parameter GQD. Displays a TEM image of GQD, showing a relatively identical size distribution between 4 and 6.5 nm according to the statistical calculation of more than 200 dots. (Fig. 2). High-resolution AFM analysis revealed that the height of GQD nanosheets is about 0.65 nm. The AFM analysis shows that the height of the quantum dot graphene layers is equal to a graphene layer (Zhu et al., 2015; Jiang et al., 2015).

The UV–Vis analysis of GQD was investigated in solvents water and ethanol at a wavelength of 200–800. The UV–Visible absorption spectrum of GQD nanoparticles in fluid is shown in Fig. 3. The spectrum of graphene absorption is 227 nm. These are related to the π-π* vibration of aromatic groups (Dong et al., 2012; Dong et al., 2015). In this figure, it can be seen that the absorption peak associated with the vibration of n-π groups of applications in GQD is greater (Dong et al., 2012).

The crystalline structures of GQD sheet were studied by XRD analysis. XRD patterns of GQD are picted in Fig. 4. Fig. 4 shows an intensive characteristic diffraction peek XRD pattern of GQD show an intensive peak (100) at 2Ө = 43.2° with interlamellar spacing of d = 12.11 Å. In addition, the XRD pattern of GQD show a broad and less intensive diffraction peak (002) at 2Ө = 22.1°. The interlayer distance was calculated according to Bragg equation (Eq. (1)) as depicted below (Ramezanzadeh et al., 2015):
(1)
2dSinӨ = nλ

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the stability of GQD in a fuel blend
ce:section.ce:para: One of the challenges in nanofluids can be the long-term instability of nanoparticles in the fluid. One of the most influential factors on the properties of nanofluids is the dispersion and stability of nanoparticles within the base fluid. In general, the long-term stability of nanofluids is one of the most important factors for their industrial application. In this study, the stability of nanoparticles using static stability of GQD nanoparticles in the fuel blend over time was examined. For this purpose, samples are poured into a transparent container and settled in one place. Appearance changes of samples with the passage of time were examined through the visual method. Fig. 6 shows sample images after 12 months. Fuels number 1, 2 and 3 are respectively B10 + E2 + GQD90, B10 + E4 + GQD90 and B10 + E6 + GQD90 (Fig. 6). It can be seen that no sedimentation of nanoparticles has occurred. Actually Graphene Quantum Dot nanoparticles in the fuel blend have been completely stable after 12 months.

The process of energy liberalization for pure biodiesel fuel, is relatively less delayed due to its high number this action leads to lower peak pressure. The thermal value of biodiesel is low and that causes a decrease in mean effective pressure. As a result, the force on the piston surface is reduced and the torque decreases. So, engine torque is less when pure biodiesel is used instead of pure diesel. On the other hand, the presence of biodiesel (about 5% to 20%) in the diesel fuel causes improvement in the combustion process, thus more energy is released and this increases the average pressure in the combustion chamber, which is a factor in increasing the power and torque produced. Also, ethanol has a lower cetane number and a higher octane number, that causes pre-ignition intensity and as a result, fast liberation of fuel energy (Jahanbakhshi and Salehi, 2019). According to Fig. 7 , we find that by adding GQD + bioethanol to B10, torque increases. The highest torques at 1800, 2100 and 2400 rpm belong to the B10 + E6 + GQD90 fuel. Torque increases at the speeds of 1800, 2100 and 2400 rpm for B10 + E6 + GQD90 fuel is 12.92%, 15.65% and 15.42% compared to D100 and is 13.79%, 16.03% and 17.30% respectively, compared to B10. The reason for the increase is the unique properties of GQD (Heidari-Maleni et al., 2020a; Heydari-Maleney et al., 2017; Lin et al., 2014). GQD has a smaller size than nanoparticles which causes better dispersal in the fuel and thus more complete combustion.

Fig. 8 shows the effect of adding GQD + bioethanol to B10 on the engine power. The highest power at the 1800, 2100 and 2400 rpm is related to B10 + E6 + GQD90. The power for the B10 + E6 + GQD90 sample was increased by 16.11%, 16.44% and 14.03% compared with the D100 and 22.94%, 20% and 22.31% compared to the B10 at 1800, 2100 and 2400 rpm, respectively. Increased engine power can be attributed to GQD + bioethanol added to the B10. Enough oxygen in GQD and bioethanol cause improved engine combustion and as a result increase in engine power. This increase can also generate energy inside the cylinder by increasing the surface-to-volume ratio of GQD and the heat transfer coefficient attributed. It was also reported that the added nanoparticle in fuel improves power and torque, and the reason is that adding nanoparticles, causes better mixing of air and fuel, thus leading to better oxygenation of the combustion chamber and more complete combustion, as result of which torque and power improve. The other researchers have reported similar results in this field (Heidari-Maleni et al., 2020a; Valihesari et al., 2019; Mirzajanzadeh et al., 2015).

According to Fig. 9 the best result of SFC was for the B10 + E6 + GQD90 sample at the speeds of 1800, 2100 and 2400 rpm. Reduction rates of SFC for the B10 + E6 + GQD90 sample were 10.98%, 19.48% and 21.13% compared to D100 and were 5.86%, 15.14% and 15.51% compared to B10 at the speeds of 1800, 2100 and 2400 rpm, respectively, Generally, adding GQD to B10 improves combustion, increases power and reduces SFC. The addition of ethanol does not have much effect on SFC, because ethanol has a lower density and heating value compared to diesel but biodiesel has a higher density and a lower thermal value compared with diesel. The results of the experiments show that the high density cannot be compensated with low thermal value. The reason for the decrease in SFC can be related to adding nanoparticles to B10 that leads to more heat release, reduction of ignition delay time that leads to more complete combustion and reduction of viscosity which leads to increased engine power (Pradhan and Singh, 2020; Parida et al., 2020).

CO is a colorless, odorless and tasteless gas which is the most abundant pollutant in the atmosphere. In general, CO is the result of incomplete combustion of fossil fuels including insufficient oxygen, low flame temperature which is produced in place of CO2 in one of the conditions mentioned. Fig. 10 shows the effect of adding GQD + bioethanol to B10 on CO emission. According to the Fig. 10, the lowest CO emissions at speeds of 1800 and 2100 rpm is related to the B10 + E6 + GQD90 sample and the lowest CO at 2400 rpm is for the B10 + E4 + GQD90 sample. Reduction of CO emissions for the B10 + E6 + GQD90 sample was 25.40% and 31% compared with D100 and 9% and 25.86% compared with B10 at the speeds of 1800 and 2100 rpm respectively and for the B10 + E4 + GQD90 sample at 2400 rpm it was 35.61% and 27.69% compared to D100 and B10, respectively. As a result, adding GQD + bioethanol to B10 reduces CO emission. One of the reasons for reducing CO is the percentage of enough oxygen by adding GQD + bioethanol in the fuel.

Fig. 11 shows the effect of adding GQD + bioethanol to B10 on CO2 emission. As can be seen from the figure, the highest CO2 emission belonged to the B10 + E4 + GQD90 sample at 1800 rpm and and at the speeds of 2100 and 2400 rpm, it was related to the B10 + E6 + GQD90 sample. The increase in CO2 for the B10 + E4 + GQD90 sample at the speed of 1800 rpm was 20% compared with D100 and was 17.17% compared with B10. For the B10 + E4 + GQD90 fuel, increase in CO2 at 2100 and 2400 rpm, compared with D100 was 30.20% and 32.65% and compared with B10 was 28.20% and 31.97%, respectively. Finally, comparing the results of CO and CO2, it can be seen that the molecular component and the equilibrium behavior are inverse. By comparing CO and CO2 we can see that equilibrium behavior between CO and CO2 is inverse. For example, CO2 for B10 + E6 + GQD90 fuel is maximum and the amount of CO is minimum for these fuel. It can be concluded that exhaust carbon has the potential to become CO2.

The main reason for which UHC is emitted is incomplete combustion. Actually, UHC is the fuel that has not taken part in the combustion process. Therefore, one of the methods used to reduce UHC emission is complete combustion in the combustion chamber. As seen in Fig. 12 , the least rate of UHC emission related to B10 + E6 + GQD90 sample at all speeds. The reductions of UHC emission for the B10 + E6 + GQD90 sample were 32.14%, 40.12% and 43.18% compared with D100 and 31.63%, 22.40% and 35.48% compared with B10 at the speeds of 1800, 2100 and 2400, respectively. Due to the fact that biodiesel has more oxygen than diesel fuel, so the combustion is better and more complete. As a result, the amount of UHC pollutants while using biodiesel is less than that when diesel fuel is employed. Adding nanoparticles with diesel/biodiesel blend improves engine combustion. As a result, the most of carbons burn and UHC decrease. Distribution of nanoparticles in fuel improves the heat transfer properties. In other words, it increases the thermal conductivity of the fuel. In general, additives change the course of the reaction and cause more complete combustion resulting in more energy release (Hoseini et al., 2020a; Heidari-Maleni et al., 2020b; Mirbagheri et al., 2020; Hoseini et al., 2020b; Wang et al., 2020).

Nitrogen oxide (NOx) pollutants depends on several factors, including cylinder internal temperature, compression ratio, oxygen content, and time required for combustion inside the cylinder (Hoseini et al., 2020a). Fig. 13 shows the effect of different fuels on NOx emissions. Using of nanoparticles improves combustion and increases the cylinder gas pressure, which increases the combustion temperature. Finally, NOx emissions increases. In addition, high thermal conductivity and high surface area of GQD improves combustion and increases the cylinder gas pressure. The lowest NOx emissions for B10 + E4 + GQD90, B10 + E2 + GQD90 and B10 + E6 + GQD90 samples at 1800, 2100 and 2400 rpm, respectively. Reductions of NOx emission for B10 + E4 + GQD90, B10 + E2 + GQD90 and B10 + E6 + GQD90 at the speeds of 1800, 2100 and 2400 rpm were respectively 0.83%, 4.61% and 2.89% compared to D100, and 3.47%, 3.87% and 5.63%, compared to B10.

Fig. 14 shows the average performance of the engine, including power, torque and SFC. On average, the highest power and torque belonged to B10 + E6 + GQD90. The lowest SFC on average related to B10 + E6 + GQD90. Compared to D100 and B10, the rates of increase in power for the B10 + E6 + GQD90 fuel were respectively 15.69% and 21.94%, torque increase rates were 15.39% and 16.26% and the rates of decrease in SFC were 17.58% and 12.52%.

Fig. 15 shows the average of exhaust emissions (CO, CO2, UHC, and NOx). The average of the lowest UHC missions is related to the B10 + E6 + GQD90 sample. The highest average of CO2 is related to B10 + E6 + GQD90. The rates of decrease in CO for B10 + E6 + GQD90 were 30.30% and 20.68%, the rates of increase in CO2 for B10 + E6 + GQD90 were 24.35% and 23.07%, and the rates of decrease in UHC for B10 + E2 + GQD90 were 38.91% and 25.13%. Decrease rates in NOx for B10 + E2 + GQD90 were 1.54% and 0.25% compared to D100 and B10, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The average effect of adding GQD + bioethanol to B10 and D100 fuels
ce:section.ce:para:
Figs. 16 and 17
show the average effect of adding GQD and bioethanol to B10 and D100 fuel on performance and exhaust emissions of the diesel engine, respectively. According to Figs. 16 and 17, we can say that the addition of GQD + bioethanol to fuel has a significant effect on the performance and exhaust emissions of the diesel engine.

An important factor in changing the properties of materials at the nanoscale is surface effects and generally overshadows all physical and chemical properties of materials. Chemical reactions take place at a point where matter is in contact with its surroundings, which is the surface of matter. One of the factors affecting the reactivity is the ratio of surface to high volume of nanoparticles. GQD nanoparticles have a very high surface-to-volume ratio, so the two quantum factors and the surface-to-volume ratio of GQD used in this research have been influential factors on the combustion process. Therefore, by adding GQD nanoparticles to the fuel, more energy is released in the fuel and more complete combustion takes place. These factors improve engine performance and reduce exhaust emissions.

This study evaluated the engine performances and emission characteristics of added GQD+ bioethanol f with concentrations of 90 ppm in B10 (D100, B10, B10 + E2 + GQD90, B10 + E4 + GQD90, B10 + E6 + GQD90, B10 + E8 + GQD90). The experiments were conducted on a single cylinder diesel engine at three speeds of 1800, 2100, and 2400 rpm under full load mode. The major conclusions from the experiments could be summarized as follows:
Addition GQD+ bioethanol to the fuel improved torque and power.
Addition GQD+ bioethanol in B10 blends at all three speed of engine (1800, 2100 and 2400 rpm) led to decrease in SFC, in comparison with additive-free B10.
Addition of GQD+ bioethanol in fuel improved CO, UHC and NOx.
On average the highest power related to B10 + E6 + GQD90.
On average the highest torque related to B10 + E6 + GQD90.
The lowest specific fuel consumption on average related to B10 + E6 + GQD90.
The rates of increase in power for the B10 + E6 + GQD90 fuel compared to D100 and B10 were 15.69% and 21.84%, respectively.
The rates of increase in torque for the B10 + E6 + GQD90 fuel compared to D100 and B10 were 15.39% and 16.26%, respectively.
The rates of decrease in SFC for B10 + E6 + GQD90 fuel compared to D100 and B10 were 17.58% and 12.52%, respectively.
The average of the lowest UHC missions is related to B10 + E6 + GQD90. The highest average of CO2 is related to B10 + E6 + GQD90.
The rates of decrease in CO for the B10 + E6 + GQD90 fuel compared to D100 and B10 were 30.3% and 20.68%, respectively.
The rates of decrease in UHC for the B10 + E6 + GQD90 fuel compared to D100 and B10 were 38.91% and 25.13%, respectively.
The rates of decrease in NOx for the B10 + E2 + GQD90 fuel compared to D100 and B10 were 1.54% and 0.25%, respectively.
Finally, we can conclude that B10 + E6 + GQD90, is the best fuel combination with regard to improving engine performance and reducing exhaust emission.

The average of performance and exhaust emissions for B10 + E6 + GQD90 compared to D100 is shown in the Fig. 18 . It can also be concluded that GQD is very stable in fuels (more than 1 year). This is an advantage for this additive and thus it can be introduced as an industrial additive that reduces exhaust emissions.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.
